coredata.dc:title: Construction of biomass derived carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 photocatalysts with superior photocatalytic activity for methylene blue degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Yanling
Tang, Wenwen
Wang, Jiayi
Ren, Fang
Chen, Zhengyan
Sun, Zhenfeng
Ren, Peng-Gang
coredata.dc:description: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) photocatalyst has been extensively studied due to its high photocatalytic activity under UV radiation, chemical stability, and low cost. However, its photocatalytic efficiency is limited by the low harvest of visible light due to larger energy band gap (Eg =3.2 eV) and the rapid recombination of photo-generated carriers. Herein, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were utilized to decorate TiO2 via a facile hydrothermal-calcination synthesis approach, wherein NCQDs were prepared using spent coffee grounds as biomass carbon source and urea as nitrogen source. The NCQDs increase the absorption of visible light, and facilitate electron transfer and separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. A very low NCQDs content of 3 wt% (3- NCQDs/TiO2) resulted in 2.25 times faster photodegradation rate for methylene blue (MB) than pristine TiO2. 3-NCQDs/TiO2 exhibited efficient and stable photocatalytic degradation over MB with photodegradation rate of 93.1% within 60 min and more than 86% after 4 cycles. Besides, it shows enhanced degradation performance in alkaline conditions. This work provides a new attempt to boost the photocatalytic activities of the photocatalysts by biomass-derived NCQDs for water remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium dioxide
Spent coffee grounds-derived carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic degradation
Dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for fabrication process of (a) NCQDs, and (b) NCQDs/TiO2.
SEM images of (a) TiO2, (b) 1-NCQDs/TiO2, (c) 3-NCQDs/TiO2, (d) 5-NCQDs/TiO2, EDS mapping (e-i) of 3-NCQDs/TiO2, inset of e is EDS spectrum, TEM (j) and HRTEM (k, l) images of 3-NCQDs/TiO2.
(a) XRD patterns of TiO2 and 3-NCQDs/TiO2, (b) FTIR spectra of NCQDs, TiO2, 3-NCQDs/TiO2.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, and core-level spectra of (b) Ti 2p, (c) O 1 s, (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s for 3-NCQDs/TiO2.
(a) PL spectra excited at a wavelength of 380 nm, (b) transient fluorescence decay curves (c) transient photocurrent responses and (d) EIS Nyquist plots of TiO2 and 3-NCQDs/TiO2.

Time-dependent absorbance of MB dye after photocatalytic degradation by (a) TiO2, (b) 1-NCQDs/TiO2, (c) 3-NCQDs/TiO2, (d) 5-NCQDs/TiO2, (e) degradation dynamics curves of four photocatalysts and (f) degradation rate of the photocatalysts.
(a) Characteristic absorption of MB after degradation for 30 min by 3-NCQDs/TiO2 under different pH conditions, (b) the corresponding dye degradation efficiency.
Cycling tests of MB degradation by 3-NCQDs/TiO2.
Mechanism for the degradation of MB by NCQDs/TiO2 under solar irradiation.

head.ce:title: Construction of biomass derived carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 photocatalysts with superior photocatalytic activity for methylene blue degradation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital media Technology, Xi’an University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) photocatalyst has been extensively studied due to its high photocatalytic activity under UV radiation, chemical stability, and low cost. However, its photocatalytic efficiency is limited by the low harvest of visible light due to larger energy band gap (Eg =3.2 eV) and the rapid recombination of photo-generated carriers. Herein, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were utilized to decorate TiO2 via a facile hydrothermal-calcination synthesis approach, wherein NCQDs were prepared using spent coffee grounds as biomass carbon source and urea as nitrogen source. The NCQDs increase the absorption of visible light, and facilitate electron transfer and separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. A very low NCQDs content of 3 wt% (3- NCQDs/TiO2) resulted in 2.25 times faster photodegradation rate for methylene blue (MB) than pristine TiO2. 3-NCQDs/TiO2 exhibited efficient and stable photocatalytic degradation over MB with photodegradation rate of 93.1% within 60 min and more than 86% after 4 cycles. Besides, it shows enhanced degradation performance in alkaline conditions. This work provides a new attempt to boost the photocatalytic activities of the photocatalysts by biomass-derived NCQDs for water remediation.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Spent coffee grounds-derived carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization and society, humans are facing the increasingly serious challenge of energy depletion and environmental pollution[1,2]. Aquatic contamination poses a severe threat to the health of ecosystems and humans[3]. In particular, organic dyes, easily recognized by their obvious color, are the major pollutants in aquatic environments and can generate dangerous byproducts through oxidation, hydrolysis, or other chemical reactions taking place in the wastewater phase[4]. Therefore, the efficient removal or degradation of pollutants has become a research hotspot for pollution abatement and environment remediation. Among various types of conventional strategies including physical adsorption, coagulation, and biodegradation, photocatalysis stands out as a compelling technique for coupling environmental remediation with energy conversion, which converts inexhaustible solar energy into strong chemical energy and degradates pollutions into more benign products without secondary pollution[5–7].

In this context, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has now been extensively studied in photocatalysis due to its high photocatalytic activity under UV radiation, chemical stability, and low cost. However, its photocatalytic efficiency is limited by the low harvest of visible light due to larger energy band gap (Eg =3.2 eV) and the rapid recombination of carriers[1,6,8,9]. In order to ameliorate these shortcomings, a variety of methods have been adopted to optimize TiO2, including metal/non-metal doping[10–12], metal deposition[13,14], and coupling with other materials to construct hybrids[15–18]. One of the emerging strategies to bridge these issues of TiO2 is conjugating it with fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs)[19–23]. CQDs, a new type of small (<10 nm) carbon nanomaterial, show excellent performance in the photocatalytic applications[24,25] owing to their ease of synthesis and surface functionalization, adjustable composition, and low toxicity[26]. Notably, they possess both up- and down-converted photoluminescence (PL), and electron-accepting and -transport properties[23]. The former enables the visible and near-infrared spectrum of sunlight to be utilized[27], and the latter restrains the recombination of electron-hole pairs, resulting in the enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, CQDs have been used as attractive photocatalysts[28,29] and modifiers to boost the photocatalytic performance of diverse semiconductors such asC3N4 [30–32], BiOIO3 [33], BiVO4 [34], BiOBr[35], and FeOOH[36] etc., since first exploitation of CQDs in the photocatalytic field by Li et al. in 2010[37]. In previous reports[38,39], the carbon sources of CQDs were usually chemical sources or graphene, which cannot meet the needs of environmental protection and low cost[40]. In this regard, biomass is advantageous as a potential carbon source for CQDs preparation in terms of widespread distribution, abundant resources, environmental friendliness, renewability, low cost, and excellent performances[41]. There are a large amount of spent coffee grounds produced in the restaurant industry every year, which have been used as carbon source to produce active carbon for electromagnetic interference shielding[42], energy storage[43] applications in our previous researches. Spent coffee grounds may be further developed as carbon source for synthesizing CQDs, which can not only improve the utilization rate and economic added-value, but also expand their applications. Moreover, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) can further improve electron transfer and expand the absorption region of the visible light by doping with N atoms[44–46].

In this work, NCQDs were prepared via hydrothermal method using spent coffee grounds as biomass carbon source and urea as nitrogen source, and then a series of NCQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts were prepared by calcination method. The introduction of NCQDs can reduce the band gap width of TiO2, significantly increase the absorption of visible light, facilitate transfer and separation of photo-generated carriers, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity. The optimal 3-NCQDs/TiO2 exhibits stable and efficient photocatalytic performance for MB with the photodegradation rate of 93.1% at 60 min, and retention of 86% after 4 cycles. Besides, it shows enhanced degradation performance in alkaline conditions.

TiO2 nano-particles, ethanol, urea and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Spent coffee grounds (SCGs, 200 mesh) were purchased from Starbucks Coffee Company Shanghai branch (Shanghai, China). Methylene blue (MB) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All the reagents were commercially available and used without further purifications. Distilled water was used throughout all tests, experiments, treatments, and rinsing.

NCQDs was prepared via the hydrothermal method using SCGs and urea as the precursors and the preparation process is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1a. Typically, SCGs was pulverized first, then ultrasonically treated in 1 M hydrochloric acid solution for 4 h, rinsed and dried. 5 g treated SCGs and 5 g urea were added into 60 mL distilled water with ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Subsequently, the dispersion was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 180 ◦C for 12 h. Finally, the undispersed solids were removed by filtration via an aqueous phase filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm, centrifugation (10,000 rpm). Finally, the supernatant was dried to obtain NCQDs.

The preparation of NCQDs/TiO2 was shown in Fig. 1b. Specifically, 1 g TiO2 and different amounts of NCQDs (1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%) were added into 60 mL ethanol and ultrasonically treated for 1 h. Subsequently, the obtained mixture was magnetically stirred continually at 75 ℃ to evaporate the ethanol, then calcined at 450 ◦C for 3 h under N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 ℃/min. Accordingly, the obtained NCQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts were denoted as 1-NCQDs/TiO2, 3-NCQDs/TiO2 and 5-NCQDs/TiO2, respectively. Additionally, the color of NCQDs/TiO2 samples changes from white of TiO2 to brown with the introduction of NCQDs.

The crystal structures of as-prepared photocatalysts were investigated by powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) using a X′Pert PRO MPD (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-7000, Japan, Cu Kα). The morphologies were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, JSM 7800 F, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, Netherlands). Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was used to analyze the distribution of elements on the NCQDs/TiO2 surface. X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) was used to determine the chemical status of the samples (K-Alpha X, Thermo Fisher, US). The functional groups of the samples were identified by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700, US). The content of NCQDs was analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, SDT Q600 V20.9 Build 20) in air atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min up to 800 °C. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the materials were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength at 380 nm by Edinburgh F-7100 FL spectrophotometer. The UV-Vis diffused reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the samples were obtained by PerkinElmer (lambda-1050) spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere assembly employing BaSO4 as reference. Photocurrent response analysis of the catalyst was carried out under intermittent light (a 300 W xenon lamp) irradiation with 30 s intervals in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CS350H, Wuhan) at room temperature.

The photocatalytic activity of the TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts were measured by the photodegradation of 10 mg L−1 MB under irradiation of simulated sunlight (300-W Xenon lamp, PLS-SXE300/300UV). In a typical experiment, 0.01 g photocatalyst was put into 100 mL 10 mg L−1 MB solution. Before irradiation, the suspensions were magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, the light was turned on and aliquots of the suspension were collected and centrifuged to remove the catalyst particulates for analysis at given irradiation time intervals (0, 10, 20 and 30 min). The residual concentration of MB was determined at 664 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The degradation rate (Dr) of MB solution is calculated from Eq. (1) as follows:
(1)
D
r
= ( 1 −
c c
) * 100 %
Where c is the concentration of the solution for each irradiated time, which was calculated from the absorbance and standard curve of MB, c
0 is the initial concentration of the solution.

To explore the effect of pH values on the photocatalytic degradation, the degradation was carried out under the identical condition as before, just with the initial pH of the MB solution being adjusted by HCl or NaOH.

A four-cycle experiment was performed to investigate the stability and reusability of the photocatalyst for degradating MB under UV–vis light irradiation under the same reaction conditions. After each cycle, the used NCQDs/TiO2 was regenerated by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and distilled water, and then dried before being reused.

The morphologies of the as-prepared samples were obtained by SEM. According to Fig. 2a-d, TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 present identical spherical shape except that the surface becomes rougher with the increase of NCQDs, which could enhance the light-harvesting and the contact with pollutants. EDS spectrum inset in Fig. 2e reveals the presence of Ti, O, C and N in 3-NCQDs/TiO2. In addition, the elemental mappings of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 in Fig. 2f-i show the uniform distribution of Ti, O, C and N elements, confirming the successful fabrication of NCQDs/TiO2.

As illustrated in Fig. 2j-l, the TEM and HRTEM images of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 show that the average size of NCQDs/TiO2 is approximately 50 nm and NCQDs with a diameter of approximately 8 nm are uniformly distributed on the TiO2 surface, which is consistent with the size of bare NCQDs illustrated in Fig. S1. The NCQDs can be clearly distinguished as dark regions, marked by yellow circles in the TEM images. Besides, the interplanar spacings of 0.35 nm and 0.34 nm in Fig. 2k and l correspond to the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2 [47] and (002) crystal plane of graphite[48,49], respectively. The direct observation of NCQDs and TiO2 from the HRTEM images indicates the successful combination of NCQDs and TiO2.

To investigate the crystal structure information of the catalysts, XRD was used to characterize the catalysts as displayed in Fig. 3a. There is little difference in the diffraction patterns of TiO2 and 3-NCQDs/TiO2 and the characteristic diffraction peaks of both samples can be well indexed as Anatase TiO2 (PDF No.21–1272). Nevertheless, no characteristic peak for CQDs is observed in the pattern of the 3-NCQDs/TiO2 due to the high dispersion, low amount of carbon quantum dots and relatively weak diffraction intensity[50]. This reveals that the introduction of NCQDs barely affects the crystal structure of TiO2.

Fig. 3b displays the FT-IR spectra of NCQDs, TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 composite. For NCQDs, the characteristic peaks located at 3100–3600 cm−1, 2940 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H, C-H and CC/CN[51]. The small peak at about 1460 cm−1 was assigned to CO in aromatic hydrocarbon. The characteristic peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibration of C-OH and C-O-C. These results signify the presence of N elements on CQDs and the plentiful functional groups of NCQDs, providing a chemical base for the binding of other molecules[19]. For TiO2, the wide absorption region below 1000 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of the Ti-O-Ti bond[52]. The peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 are ascribed to O-H stretching vibration and bending vibration of the adsorbed water on the surface of the samples. The spectrum of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 roughly consists with that of TiO2 together with weak characteristic peaks of NCQDs, further confirming the successful combination of NCQDs and TiO2. Moreover, the broadening and red shift of the absorption region below 1000 cm−1 is owing to the combination of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C, indicating the formation of Ti-O-C bonding interaction between TiO2 and NCQDs, which is beneficial to the uniform and tight dispersion of NCQDs on TiO2, as well as the transfer of photo-induced electrons.

X-ray photoelectron spectrums (XPS) of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 was examined to explore the chemical composition and chemical state. Fig. 4a displays the XPS survey spectrum, and the peaks at 284.8, 399.1, 459.0 and 530.1 eV belong to C 1 s, N 1 s, Ti 2p and O 1 s, consistent with the EDS and FTIR results. In the Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 4b), there are two distinct peaks at 458.7 and 464.5 eV, corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively with a slit of 5.8 eV, implying the dominant Ti4+ cation in the form of TiO2。The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4c exhibits three distinct peaks at 529.6, 530.1 and 531.0 eV, which could be ascribed to Ti-O, CO and C-O-H, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4d can be fitted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.7 and 288.4 eV, belonging to C-C[33], C-O/C-N and CO/CN bonds from NCQDs. The characteristic peaks at the binding energies of 398.7 and 400.3 eV in N 1 s (Fig. 4e) are associated with C-N-C[53] and N-H bonds, respectively. The XPS results are consistent with the analysis of FTIR spectroscopies.

In order to determine the exact amount of NCQDs loaded on TiO2, thermo-gravimetric analysis of NCQDs/TiO2 composites were conducted as shown in Fig. S2. There are two stages of weight losses for all composites that can be observed in the TGA curves. The weight loss below 250 °C is assigned to the evaporation of adsorbed water. Between 250 °C and 800 °C, the weight loss can be ascribed to the combustion of NCQDs. Therefore, the contents of NCQDs in 1-NCQDs/TiO2, 3-NCQDs/TiO2 and 5-NCQDs/TiO2 are ca.2.4%, 3.5%, and 4.9%, respectively, which are roughly in proportion to the adding amount of NCQDs in the preparation process.

The photoabsorption behaviors of TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 were investigated by testing the UV–visible light absorbance spectra, as shown in Fig. 5a. It is evidently observed that the absorption edge of TiO2 is below 400 nm, suggesting that the TiO2 is exclusively activated by UV( light wavelength (λ)< 387 nm)[54]. The incorporation of NCQDs into the TiO2 matrix leads to an increase in UV–vis absorption over the entire wavelength through upconversion luminescence properties[30,37], thereby enhancing the availability of visible light, stimulating the generation of more charge carriers and improving the photocatalytic activity. With the introduction of NCQDs, the color of pristine TiO2 changes from white to brown, which is consistent with the changes of their optical absorption. The energy gap ( Eg ) of TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 was measured by Kubelka-Munk method[55], and the calculation formula is as follows:
(2)
α hv
2
= A
hv − Eg
Where α is the light absorption index, h is the Planck constant, v is the frequency,
A
is the absorbance, and
Eg
is the semiconductor forbidden band width.The plots of (
α hv
)2 vs. hv are shown in Fig. 5b and Eg of TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 were calculated from the Tauc plot of (αhν)2 versus hν, in which the curve was extrapolated to the X-axis to yield the band gap of the material. The extracted
Eg
values of TiO2 and 3-NCQDs/TiO2 are 3.20 and 2.90 eV, respectively. The widened light-responsive region and reduced band gap of NCQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst are more conducive to the sunlight harvest and the generation of photo-generated carriers, further leading to an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the valence band (VB) positions of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 measured by VB XPS was 2.60 eV (Fig. 5c). Based on Eg value and VB position, the corresponding conduction band (CB) potential was calculated to be − 0.30 eV. The estimated band positions of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 were proposed in Fig. 5d.

The PL spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm to explore the recombination capacity of photo-generated electron-holes by following the rule of the positive correlation of the intensity and the capacity[56]. As shown in Fig. 6a, both samples possess a broad emission peak at 460–470 nm, which is in accordance with previous reported TiO2 based photocatalysts[17,20,47], and the PL intensity of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 was distinctly lower than that of pure TiO2, indicating the effective promotion of the separation of photo-generated electron-holes pairs by NCQDs[21,38,57], thus endowing 3-NCQDs/TiO2 with enhanced photocatalytic activity[58]. Transient fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on TiO2 and 3-NCQDs/TiO2 to elucidate the charge transfer kinetics, as shown in Fig. 6b. After excitation, PL decay dynamics appeared to follow a single exponential growth curve after first 4.8 ns, exhibiting exponential decay and the average exciton lifetimes of TiO2 and 3-NCQDs/TiO2 are 2.22 and 3.13 ns, indicating that 3-NCQDs/TiO2 shows effectively retarded recombination of photoinduced charge carriers and prolonged retention, as well as enhanced photocatalytic activity. The prolonged lifetime of electron-holes pairs means the weakened PL emission intensity, which is consistent with PL results. The transient photocurrent responses of TiO2 and 3-NCQDs/TiO2 illustrated in Fig. 6c show that the photocurrent intensity of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 was significantly higher than that of TiO2, indicating 3-NCQDs/TiO2 possesses more effective photogenerated carriers due to the increased light harvesting ability and charge transfer efficiency. Fig. 6d describes the electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS), the lower arc of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 in the EIS Nyquist plot means lower charge-transfer resistance, thus promoting effective separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. As mentioned above, the introduction of NCQDs can enhance the visible light absorption performance, improve the separation of e--h+ pairs, so NCQDs/TiO2 have predictable enhanced photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared photocatalysts were evaluated by the degradation of MB solutions under 300 W xenon lamp and the corresponding results are given in Fig. 7. For MB degradation, the decrease of the dye concentration over time was monitored by measuring the absorbance of the characteristic MB absorption peak (Fig. 7a-d). TiO2 exhibits slow degradation for MB with a degradation rate of 94.8% after 110 min, while the degradation rates of 1-NCQDs/TiO2, 3-NCQDs/TiO2 and 5-NCQDs/TiO2 are 92.1%, 93.1% and 89.3% after 70, 60 and 100 min, respectively. From Fig. 7e, it can be observed that 3-NCQDs/TiO2 exhibits the most rapid degradation for MB with a degradation rate of 93.1% within 60 min, higher than that of TiO2 (74.0%), 1-NCQDs/TiO2 (89.8%), and 5-NCQDs/TiO2 (79.3%). To clarify the quantitative characterization of MB degradation kinetics by different photocatalysts, the kinetic plots (
ln
c
/
c
0
) (here,
c
0
is the absorbed equilibrium MB concentration, c is the MB concentration at time t) as a function of irradiation time were sketched for TiO2, 1-NCQDs/TiO2, 3-NCQDs/TiO2 and 5-NCQDs/TiO2, which show good linear fitting (Fig. 7f). The degradation rate constant (k) can be computed from the slope of the linear plots. k of 3-NCQDs/TiO2 is as high as 0.0454 min−1, which is approximately 2.25, 1.19 and 1.59 times that of TiO2 (0.0202 min−1), 1-NCQDs/TiO2 (0.0383 min−1), and 5-NCQDs/TiO2 (0.0285 min−1).

To investigate the application potential of this catalytic system, the effect of the initial pH on MB degradation by the optimal 3-NCQDs/TiO2 was investigated in the pH range of 2.8–11 ( Fig. 8a-b). It could be noted that the degradation rate increases with the increase of the pH, which is 53.7%, 80.2% and 99.8% at pH= 2.8, 7 and 11 after irradiation for 30 min. The reason for the increased degradation activity may be the promotion of·OH generation in alkaline conditions for MB degradation. Meanwhile, OH- can react with photogenerated holes and restrain the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The inferior photocatalysis in acid condition could be attributed to less OH- under acidic condition. From the perspective of practical applications, it still needs to develop a photocatalyst with satisfactory degradation behaviors regardless of alkalinity or acidity in further researches.

A four-cycle experiment was performed on degradating MB by 3-NCQDs/TiO2 under UV–vis light irradiation under the same reaction conditions. After each cycle, the used 3-NCQDs/TiO2 was regenerated by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and distilled water, and then dried before being reused. As exhibited in Fig. 9, the degradation rate can still achieve 86% after 4 repeated cycles, demonstrating high recyclability and practicability of 3-NCQDs/TiO2.

Based on the above research, we proposed a possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of MB by NCQDs/TiO2, which is illustrated in Fig. 10. When being irradiated by sunlight, the electrons in the valence band (VB) of TiO2 will absorbs photon energy from solar radiation and excites electron (e-) hole (h+) pairs in the CB and VB, respectively. e- is easily oxidized by dissolved oxygen in water to form •O2 -. In addition, holes can also oxidize OH- and H2O molecules adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 to produce •OH[4]. However, the e- and h+ readily recombine with low activity. The introduction of NCQDs can effectively increase the harvest of visible light and facilitate charge transfer. As a result, more photocarriers are generated and their recombination is markedly suppressed, leading to a significant increase of efficient active radicals[26]. Therefore, under the co-function of h+, •O2 - and •OH, MB molecules were mineralized to produce CO2 and H2O.

NCQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts were prepared via a facile hydrothermal-calcination approach, wherein NCQDs were prepared via hydrothermal method using spent coffee grounds as biomass carbon source and urea as nitrogen source. The as-prepared NCQDs/TiO2 has reduced band gap, broadened light response range, higher light absorption capacity, and enhanced separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers. Benefited from the multiple advantages, NCQDs/TiO2 exhibits high photocatalytic activity for MB degradation than pristine TiO2. The optimal 3-NCQDs/TiO2 exhibits stable and efficient photocatalytic performance for MB with the photodegradation rate of 93.1% at 60 min, and retention of 86% after 4 cycles. Besides, it shows higher degradation activity in alkaline conditions.

Yanling Jin: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wenwen Tang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Data curation. Jiayi Wang: Formal analysis. Fang Ren: Data curation, Funding acquisition. Zhengyan Chen: Validation. Zhenfeng Sun: Resources. Peng-Gang Ren: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51773167, 52102303, 52103095), Foundation of Education Department of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 20JK0805), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2019M650268), and the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering (Sichuan University) (Grant No. sklpme2020–4-16, sklpme2021–05-09),

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.167627.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding TiO2 photocatalysis: mechanisms and materials
Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Schneider, M. Matsuoka, M. Takeuchi, J.L. Zhang, Y. Horiuchi, M. Anpo, D.W. Bahnemann, Understanding TiO2 Photocatalysis: Mechanisms and Materials, Chem. Rev., 114 (2014) 9919–9986.
